schools.summary: A denunciation of the oppressive rich (not all rich, but the wicked who hoard and defraud) — 'go to now, ye rich men, weep and howl' (not the weeping of repentance but the loud howling of anguish, a prophetic picture of terror, Ellicott) 'for your miseries that shall come upon you' (God's judgments soon to fall); not an exhortation to repent but a denunciation meant to terrify (Calvin), spoken chiefly for the comfort of the oppressed poor (Wesley).

The rotting of hoarded wealth — 'your riches are corrupted' (sesepen, perfect, already putrefied; the ancients' riches largely stores of grain, wine, oil that spoil when hoarded, Clarke) 'and your garments are motheaten' (costly wardrobes laid up in vain superfluity, eaten while the poor went unclothed); wealth heaped up and withheld decays, its accumulation beyond use its ruin; ridiculing their confidence in fading riches (Calvin).

The rust that testifies — 'your gold and silver is cankered' (katiotai, rusted through from disuse; though pure gold does not truly corrode, the figure for wealth so hoarded it might as well rust, Ellicott); 'the rust of them shall be a witness against you' (the corrosion bearing witness in God's court to selfish hoarding) and 'shall eat your flesh as fire' (the rust become a figure of consuming judgment); 'ye have heaped treasure for the last days' (amassing perishable wealth when they should lay up in heaven).

themes: heaped treasure for the last days

schools.summary: The defrauded labourer's cry — 'the hire of the labourers... kept back by fraud' (the rich withholding or delaying the wages of those who reaped their fields, breaking the law requiring payment the same day, Lev 19:13; Deut 24:15); the wages personified 'crieth' to heaven, and 'the cries of them which have reaped are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth' (the Lord of hosts, of irresistible power, hearing the oppressed); sins human laws ignore crying louder to God (Wesley).

Wanton luxury — 'ye have lived in pleasure' (etryphesate, a soft, delicate, voluptuous life) 'and been wanton' (espatalesate, lived lasciviously without restraint), fed by the fraud just named; 'ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter' (pampered and fattened themselves like cattle gorged before the kill, their rich living unknowingly fitting them for the slaughter of judgment); the grim irony — the day of feasting the day of slaughter.

themes: as in a day of slaughter

schools.summary: The climax of guilt — 'ye have condemned and killed the just' (the rich, controlling the courts, procuring the legal condemnation and death of the righteous poor, by violence or perverted justice); 'and he doth not resist you' (the just one, powerless, offers no resistance, his helplessness aggravating their cruelty and leaving his cause to God).

debates.question: Who is 'the just' that they killed?

sides.position: the righteous poor generally — the class of innocent victims the oppressive rich destroyed (singular used collectively)
an allusion to 'that Just One,' Christ (Acts 3:14), and the righteous like James the Just

schools.summary: The call to patience — 'be patient therefore' (makrothymesate, a corollary from the coming judgment on the rich, the assurance of judgment the ground of patience) 'unto the coming of the Lord' (his advent to judge, the imminent judgment on Jerusalem and his final coming); the husbandman's illustration — he 'waiteth for the precious fruit... the early and latter rain' (waiting with long patience through the seasons, unable to hurry the crop); if the farmer waits for corn, will not the believer for a crown? (Henry).

themes: be patient unto the coming of the Lord

schools.summary: The call to steadfast hearts — 'be ye also patient' (as the husbandman waits, through the season of trial); 'stablish your hearts' (sterixate, make firm and resolute the inner man, not sinking under oppression; correcting the softness of heart that weakens perseverance, Calvin; take courage, Clarke); the motive 'for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh' (eggiken, his advent near, in judgment on the persecuting nation and his final coming, the ground of steadfastness).

themes: the coming of the Lord draweth nigh

schools.summary: The warning against murmuring — 'grudge not one against another' (better 'murmur/groan not,' me stenazete; under shared affliction believers grow fretful and vent distress in complaints against their brethren, an inward groan of ill-humour breaking out); 'lest ye be condemned' (such censoriousness itself a fault God will judge); 'the judge standeth before the door' (the Lord at hand, hearing every word, his nearness a check on murmuring); leave wrongs in God's hands (Clarke).

themes: the judge standeth before the door

schools.summary: The prophets as patterns — 'take the prophets for an example' (hypodeigma, a copy to imitate, John 13:15; pointing the readers to those who trod the same path); their dignity 'who have spoken in the name of the Lord' (God's honoured spokesmen, yet not exempt from affliction); the double lesson 'suffering affliction, and of patience' (enduring grievous persecution and bearing it patiently); the very people who gloried in having prophets persecuted them (Wesley); the sufferer in noble company.

themes: take the prophets for an example
spoken in the name of the Lord

schools.summary: Job's endurance and the Lord's merciful end — 'we count them happy which endure' (blessed those who hold out under suffering, echoing Christ's beatitude, Mt 5:11; trial the path to blessedness); 'the patience of Job' (no one overwhelmed with troubles so hard, yet he kept his integrity, Calvin); 'the end of the Lord' (the issue God gave, restoring and blessing his latter end); the lesson 'the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy' (the merciful outcome revealing God's compassion).

themes: we count them happy which endure
very pitiful and of tender mercy

schools.summary: The warning against swearing — 'above all things... swear not' (drawn almost verbatim from Christ, Mt 5:34-37); 'neither by heaven... nor any other oath' (the rash profane swearing by creatures common among the Jews, who swore freely fancying such oaths less binding, Wesley); 'let your yea be yea' (plain truthfulness, a Christian's word as good as another's oath); 'lest ye fall into condemnation' (careless swearing profaning God's name).

debates.question: Does 'swear not' forbid all oaths or only profane swearing?

sides.position: chiefly rash, profane, conversational swearing by creatures, not the solemn judicial oath

holders: the Essenes and some sectaries (noted by Ellicott)

schools.summary: Prayer and praise for every condition — 'is any afflicted? let him pray' (the one suffering turning to God for help, not to murmuring or rash oaths); 'is any merry? let him sing psalms' (cheerfulness expressed in praise, joy sanctified heavenward); no time in which God does not invite us — affliction stimulating prayer, prosperity supplying praise (Calvin); the whole life revolving around God's throne (Ellicott); the Christian in both sorrow and gladness remembering God.

themes: is any afflicted? let him pray
is any merry? let him sing psalms

schools.summary: The care of the sick — 'is any sick? let him call for the elders of the church' (the presbyters, the recognised officers; bishop and presbyter identical in the apostolic age, Ellicott); 'let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord' (prayer the heart, anointing its accompaniment, in the Lord's authority); the oil connected with the gift of healing then in the church (Mark 6:13, Calvin/Wesley) or a medicinal use; the Reformed firmly rejecting the later sacramental 'extreme unction' for the dying — here the oil is for healing the living.
Wesley: this single conspicuous gift remained in the church after the other miraculous gifts were withdrawn, and seems designed to remain always.

debates.question: What is the anointing with oil?

sides.position: a symbol attending the gift of miraculous healing then in the church (Mark 6:13), prayer being the heart of the act
NOT the sacrament of 'extreme unction' for the dying — the oil here is for healing the living

themes: is any sick? call for the elders
in the name of the Lord

schools.summary: The power of believing prayer — 'the prayer of faith' (marked by faith, by the elders and the sick man's trust; not the oil) 'shall save the sick' (sosei, make well, restore to health, no reference to the soul's salvation here, Robertson) 'and the Lord shall raise him up' (the Lord himself the healer); the qualification — not unconditional, faith and prayer sometimes not granted recovery when it would harm the soul (Clarke), submitting to God's will; 'if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven' (pardon accompanying healing, body and soul).

themes: the Lord shall raise him up

schools.summary: Mutual confession and fervent prayer — 'confess your faults one to another' (chiefly sins against one another, openly acknowledged for reconciliation, and unburdening conscience); the Protestant expositors firm against the Roman auricular use — mutual confession among brethren, not to a priest, to any who can pray in faith (Wesley), the sacramental sense unprovable from the Greek (Ellicott); 'pray one for another, that ye may be healed'; 'the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much' (earnest prayer mighty with God).

debates.question: Does 'confess your faults one to another' support auricular confession to a priest?

sides.position: no — it enjoins mutual confession among brethren, to any who can pray in faith, not to a priest; the sacramental meaning cannot be established from the text

themes: confess your faults one to another
the fervent prayer of a righteous man

schools.summary: Elijah as proof of prayer's power — 'a man subject to like passions as we are' (homoiopathes, of the same nature and weaknesses, not a superhuman saint, so none think such prayer beyond ordinary believers); 'he prayed earnestly' (the Hebrew idiom 'with prayer he prayed,' marking intensity) 'that it might not rain: and it rained not... three years and six months' (heaven shut at the prophet's request; James supplying the detail behind 1 Kings); God making heaven subject to Elijah's prayer (Calvin).

themes: Elias a man of like passions
it rained not three years and six months

schools.summary: The second prayer restoring the rain — 'he prayed again' (the same prophet who shut heaven now opens it; the praying found where Elijah cast himself down on Carmel, face between his knees, 1 Kings 18:42); 'the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth her fruit' (the drought broken, the barren land revived to harvest); the two prayers together — one shutting, one opening heaven — the full demonstration that prayer is mighty in both directions; crowning v16.

themes: the earth brought forth her fruit

schools.summary: The closing concern for the wanderer — 'if any of you do err from the truth' (planethe, stray, whether by doctrinal error or sinful practice; the truth at once a creed to believe and a way to walk; some doctrinal mistake usually at the bottom of every practical error, Henry); 'and one convert him' (another in love turning the wanderer back, the work of restoration belonging to the brethren); the last and dearest of the apostle's remarks (Ellicott), ending not in condemnation but in hope of restoration.

themes: if any err from the truth

schools.summary: The blessedness of saving a soul — 'let him know' (be absolutely sure, for encouragement); 'he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death' (the soul, of more value than the body, rescued from eternal death); 'shall hide a multitude of sins' (the convert's many sins covered, blotted out, forgiven in his conversion; the proverb 'love covereth a multitude of sins,' Prov 10:12; 1 Pet 4:8, fulfilled); the recovery of a soul among the greatest works, the gracious close of a practical epistle.
